main_summary.summary: Steven McNichols, interviewed in March 2013 in Berkeley, California by David Cline for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts his trajectory from a New York childhood split between a 133rd Street tenement and a Dalton School scholarship to a UCLA-based civil rights career. McNichols describes participating in the September 1961 Houston Freedom Ride organized by Houston attorneys George Washington Jr. and Hayma K., bankrolled in part by Theodore Hugo Brooks and supported by the Progressive Youth Association. He recounts the group of eighteen demonstrators arrested for unlawful assembly at the Union Station coffee shop and the overnight beating administered by white prisoners in the Harris County misdemeanor tank under jail trustees Garland and Omer Street. After conviction was reversed by the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals, McNichols became Civil Rights Director of the National Student Association, organizing the 1964 Thanksgiving Fast for Freedom that financed expansion of the Department of Agriculture surplus food program across every Mississippi county. He describes lobbying for the 1964 Civil Rights Act and Voting Rights Act through the Leadership Conference on Civil Rights, work on the MFDP Congressional Strategy Committee with Rita Schwerner, a McComb, Mississippi visit at the height of the 1964 bombings, and his retrospective view that NSA's international apparatus was a CIA front directed by Bob Kiley.
key_themes: National Student Association civil rights work
main_summary.historical_significance: McNichols offers detailed first-person testimony on the Los Angeles CORE chapter of the 1961 Freedom Rides, a less-documented West Coast extension of the campaign organized by Arnold Walters's LA CORE and Houston attorneys George Washington Jr. and Hayma K. His account of the overnight beating in the Harris County misdemeanor tank, the role of trustees Garland and Omer Street, the L.A. Times article that triggered Garland's near-retaliation, and George Washington Jr.'s Polaroid documentation of injuries fills a gap in the Houston Freedom Ride record. His subsequent NSA work documents how the 1964 Thanksgiving Fast for Freedom funded the Department of Agriculture surplus food rollout to every county in Mississippi, a poverty-relief mechanism not centered in most Civil Rights Movement narratives. The interview also preserves contemporaneous observations on the NSA-CIA pipeline (Bob Kiley to Richard Helms) and on AFL-CIO anticommunist resistance to SNCC within the Leadership Conference on Civil Rights.

chapters.title: Family Origins and New York Childhood
chapters.summary: McNichols traces his maternal grandparents' Rothschild-sponsored emigration from Riga and Marijampole to Palestine, where they founded Tel Aviv and built its first residential home. He describes his mother's classical violin training in Geneva under Joseph Szigeti, his father's St. Louis upbringing and self-taught electronics career at Picker X-Ray Corporation, and his own childhood as the only white child on a Puerto Rican block at 133rd Street while attending Dalton School on full scholarship.

chapters.title: Bipolar Diagnosis and Mother's Death
chapters.summary: McNichols describes his mother's psychiatric breakdown in the Time-Life lobby when he was sixteen, his own undiagnosed bipolar condition that produced fifteen years of suicidal ideation, and the late-life lithium diagnosis at age 65 after a cardiologist's lab panel detected near-zero lithium serum levels. He recounts his mother's death from a botched brain-tumor operation after a decade of misdiagnosis.

chapters.title: UCLA Politics and NSA-CIA Pipeline
chapters.summary: McNichols arrives at UCLA in 1958, enters student politics in the Howard Berman/Henry Waxman orbit, and rises through National Student Association conferences. He retrospectively identifies the NSA international apparatus as a CIA front, naming Bob Kiley as former NSA president who became Richard Helms's special assistant at the CIA, and noting the Youth and Student Foundation as a transparent funding conduit.

chapters.title: Houston Freedom Ride Recruitment
chapters.summary: McNichols describes how Houston Black attorneys George Washington Jr. and Hayma K., with bondsman Theodore Hugo Brooks's financial backing, persuaded CORE national director James Farmer to send Freedom Riders to Houston. He volunteers through Los Angeles CORE under Arnold Walters, describes the two-day training that focused entirely on white-mob attack scenarios rather than post-arrest jail conditions, and recounts the ticketing mix-up between Southern Pacific and Union Pacific stations.

chapters.title: Houston Arrest at Union Station
chapters.summary: Eleven Freedom Riders from Los Angeles join with Progressive Youth Association members in Houston, totaling eighteen demonstrators. After meeting at a soul food restaurant, they form a line at the Union Station coffee shop, are denied entry, and are arrested for unlawful assembly when the proprietor opens the door. McNichols recounts an FBI agent who joined the train at El Paso and the cooperative Black Pullman porters who supplied the group with private car space.
related_events: Houston Union Station coffee shop sit-in

chapters.title: Harris County Jail Beating
chapters.summary: After processing at 2 a.m. in the Harris County jail, McNichols and three fellow Freedom Riders (Bob Kaufman, Steve Sanfield, Joe Stevenson) are placed in the white male misdemeanor tank with 107 prisoners and beaten under instructions from guards. Bob Kaufman's left ear is opened to a two-inch wound; Sanfield is kicked in the eye; McNichols sustains an inguinal hernia and disc injury. McNichols recounts learning the lesson of his New York childhood: lie still when overwhelmed.

chapters.title: Garland, Omer Street, and George Washington Jr.
chapters.summary: McNichols describes the trustee hierarchy that ran the misdemeanor tank: commander Garland in cell one and lieutenant Omer Street roaming the dining area. After negotiating shoes for protection with Omer Street, the Freedom Riders survive the second night. When an L.A. Times article identifying Garland as the beating perpetrator arrives, Garland goes berserk; attorney George Washington Jr. arrives, documents Bob Kaufman's wound with a Polaroid, and the supervisor de-escalates the confrontation.

chapters.title: Trial, Conviction, and Reversal
chapters.summary: McNichols describes the first trial ending in mistrial when George Washington Jr. raised a discrepancy between the indictment's reference to the union coffee shop and the actual Union Station coffee shop. The judge agreed to accommodate the defendants' UCLA fall registration in scheduling the retrial, an accommodation McNichols contrasts with Mississippi practice. Conviction at the second trial was reversed by the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals on the strength of an obscure Texas statute prohibiting racial discrimination in transportation.

chapters.title: NSA Civil Rights Director and Thanksgiving Fast
chapters.summary: Returning to UCLA, McNichols cultivates his Freedom Rider standing in NSA student politics and is named NSA Civil Rights Director, moving to Philadelphia for a year. His first major project is the 1964 Thanksgiving Fast for Freedom, which raises $38,000 from fasting students and colleges nationwide to fund Mississippi food distribution.

chapters.title: Department of Agriculture Surplus Food Expansion
chapters.summary: McNichols proposes redirecting the $38,000 from direct food purchases to a Department of Agriculture-aligned campaign that enrolled every Mississippi county in the federal surplus food program. Working with Robert W. Spike of the National Council of Churches and Art Thomas of the Delta Ministry, plus a USDA official who used the National Council of Churches and NSA as leverage, the program moved Mississippi from roughly 70 percent county participation to 100 percent, channeling food to Black residents previously excluded.

chapters.title: McComb Visit and MFDP Congressional Challenge
chapters.summary: McNichols describes his first NSA assignment: an Inter-Agency Conference of Civil Rights Organizations meeting in Atlanta followed by an SNCC-coordinated visit to McComb, Mississippi during the eleven-bombings-in-twenty-two-days period after Andrew Goodman, James Chaney, and Michael Schwerner's murders. After a rally, Jackson police escort the group out at the airport at 4 a.m. with the warning not to return. He recounts joining the MFDP Congressional Strategy Committee, becoming close to Rita Schwerner, and his National Health Law Services-funded investigation establishing White House opposition to the MFDP challenge.

chapters.title: Voting Rights Act and AFL-CIO Anticommunism
chapters.summary: McNichols recounts attending the Voting Rights Act signing ceremony, receiving a White House pen, and his Leadership Conference on Civil Rights work pushing more militant action. He closes by criticizing AFL-CIO and NAACP leadership for anticommunist hostility toward SNCC, attributing the resistance to figures whose 1930s organizing-era loyalties carried into Civil Rights Movement-era exclusion. Clarence Mitchell Jr.'s NAACP Washington office, he says, refused to share information about food-distribution meetings.
